perturbation.original_text: Furthermore, the Receiving Party hereby undertakes: (a) not to use the AMDOCS Confidential Information for any purposes other than performance of the Services; (b) not to sell, grant, make available to, or otherwise allow the use of the AMDOCS Confidential Information by any third party, directly or indirectly; and (c) not to use, directly or indirectly, the AMDOCS Confidential Information in the development and/or sale of software systems, for itself or for a third party, and/or in the provision of any services to a third party, except for the Services to be provided by the Receiving Party to SBC.
perturbation.changed_text: Furthermore, the Receiving Party should endeavor: (a) not to use the AMDOCS Confidential Information for any purposes other than performance of the Services; (b) not to sell, grant, make available to, or otherwise allow the use of the AMDOCS Confidential Information by any third party, directly or indirectly, without giving prior consideration; and (c) not to use, directly or indirectly, the AMDOCS Confidential Information in the development and/or sale of software systems, for itself or for a third party, and/or in the provision of any services to a third party, except for the Services to be provided by the Receiving Party to SBC.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'undertakes' to 'should endeavor' weakens the commitment from a strict obligation to a suggestion. The added phrase 'without giving prior consideration' to clause (b) introduces ambiguity as to what constitutes 'prior consideration,' allowing room for interpretation and potentially violating the original intent of preventing unauthorized use or transfer of information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1832 criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. By making the prohibition against selling or making AMDOCS confidential information available to third parties conditional on 'prior consideration,' the modified text introduces a loophole. This could potentially allow for the misappropriation of trade secrets if the receiving party argues they gave 'prior consideration,' even if this 'consideration' is inadequate or doesn't align with the original intent of protecting AMDOCS' proprietary information. The ambiguity weakens the protection of trade secrets, contradicting the purpose of the law.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially contradicting 18 U.S.C. § 1832. By changing 'undertakes' to 'should endeavor' and adding 'without giving prior consideration,' the new text makes the obligation less stringent and creates a loophole for misappropriation. The law snippet explicitly refers to 'Theft of trade secrets' and mentions 'Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret...to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof,' which the modified contract language could potentially allow under the guise of 'prior consideration,' thus creating a contradiction. The original text provided a stricter, enforceable obligation, whereas the modified text weakens this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed under the laws of the State of New York, USA without giving effect to its provisions regarding conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted under principles arguably similar to the laws of the State of New York, USA, with some consideration given to broader legal precedents.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'governed and construed under the laws of the State of New York' to 'interpreted under principles arguably similar to the laws of the State of New York, USA, with some consideration given to broader legal precedents' introduces significant ambiguity. It's no longer clear that New York law governs, creating uncertainty about which legal framework applies and opening the door to conflicting interpretations. Adding 'some consideration given to broader legal precedents' further muddies the waters.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York UCC § 1-301 generally allows parties to a contract to choose the law that will govern their agreement, subject to certain limitations. By making the governing law vague and uncertain, the modified text undermines the parties' ability to clearly define the legal framework for their agreement. This could lead to disputes over which laws apply, potentially violating the UCC's goal of promoting certainty and predictability in commercial transactions. If the 'broader legal precedents' contradict specific provisions of New York law that would otherwise apply, this creates a direct conflict.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 1 No title PART 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules up PART 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules next SECTION 1-302 Variation by Agreement This entry was published on 2014-12-26 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 1-301 Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 1, PART 3 Section 1--301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties so long as none of the parties to the transaction is a consumer and a resident of New York. Where a consumer is a resident of the state of New York, New York state law shall apply. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), this act applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of this act specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2--402; (2) Sections 2-A--105 and 2-A--106; (3) Section 4--102; (4) Section 4-A--507; (5) Section 5--116; (6) Section 8--110; and (7) Sections 9--301 through 9--307.

Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states the agreement is governed and construed under the laws of New York. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement will be 'interpreted under principles arguably similar to the laws of the State of New York,' which contradicts the original clear statement and undermines the parties' ability to define the legal framework as permitted by UCC § 1-301. The changed text also brings in 'broader legal precedents' which can contradict specific provisions of New York law, creating a direct conflict, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties.' The changed text deviates from this principle by suggesting a non-specific adherence to principles similar to New York law and considering broader precedents, potentially nullifying the agreement to be governed by New York law.
